title: Louie Meets The Folks

jokes.text: He knocked you out in the second round. Yeah, but now he's going around shooting off his mouth, saying he can do it in one.
jokes.setup: Tony mentions Rocky gave him a good fight and deserves another shot
jokes.punchline: Reveals Rocky knocked him out and now claims he can do it faster
jokes.explanation: Great character-driven humor showing Tony's delusional pride - he frames being knocked out as a 'good fight' and then inadvertently reveals how badly he lost.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, Rocky gave me a good fight. He deserves another shot.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Tony, Rocky knocked you out in the first round.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So what? He's still a good fighter.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Now he's telling people he can do it faster.

jokes.text: He says he had walking pneumonia or something that night, you know? Fever of 104. Oh, I hate a man who makes excuses. Me, too.
jokes.setup: Rocky supposedly had pneumonia as an excuse
jokes.punchline: Tony condemns excuses while simultaneously making one for his opponent
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing Tony's lack of self-awareness about his own contradictory logic.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know what I hate? Excuses. A man's gotta stand up, take what's comin' to him, no excuses.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: But Rocky, he's got pneumonia. The guy can barely breathe. How'm I supposed to fight a guy with pneumonia?

jokes.text: Would you please send me a photograph, a lock of your hair, one of your shirts, a pair of your shoes and a pillow you've slept on?
jokes.setup: Bobby reads what seems like a normal fan letter request
jokes.punchline: List escalates from normal photo request to increasingly creepy personal items
jokes.explanation: Well-structured escalation from normal to absurd that feels believable for an obsessed fan.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Dear Mr. Becker, I am your biggest fan. Could you send me a signed photo?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I would also love to have a lock of your hair.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: And perhaps some of your used clothing.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: A fingernail clipping would be wonderful.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: And finally, could you send me the name and address of your mother?

jokes.text: I can't believe that someone would have the nerve to ask for this stuff. I mean, the postage to send it would cost you... It came to $4.98.
jokes.setup: Elaine seems outraged at the fan's nerve
jokes.punchline: Reveals she's only concerned about the shipping cost, having calculated it
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that perfectly captures Elaine's practical mindset - she's calculated the exact shipping cost while pretending to be morally outraged.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You want me to sign this? Do you have any idea how much it costs to ship a signed photo?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: No, how much?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Forty-seven dollars! Forty-seven dollars! That's outrageous!

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, you mean the one where my face is taped to the body of Miss October?
jokes.setup: Jim mentions seeing Zena's photo in Louie's office
jokes.punchline: Reveals Louie has created a bizarre composite photo
jokes.explanation: Perfect character detail for Louie - creepy, pathetic, yet somehow endearing in its crude devotion.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I saw your photo in Louie's office.

dialogue.speaker: Zena
dialogue.line: Really? How did it look?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, it had your eyes, but the rest was made up of different women's body parts.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I was trying to create the perfect woman!

jokes.text: I think he means Zena. Oh, my mistake.
jokes.setup: Tony presumably greets Alex romantically thinking it's Zena
jokes.punchline: Casual correction after inappropriate greeting
jokes.explanation: Simple mistaken identity gag with Tony's understated reaction providing the humor.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Hey beautiful, how'd you like to come back to my place and help me sort through my record collection?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It's me, Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Look at Reiger and Ignatowski. They think you're serious. She loves me. I am serious.
jokes.setup: Louie claims Alex and Jim don't understand Zena's playful rejection
jokes.punchline: Zena confirms she actually is serious about rejecting him
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment as Louie's delusion is immediately punctured.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alex and Jim, they don't understand. When a woman says no, she doesn't always mean no.

dialogue.speaker: Zena
dialogue.line: No, Louie. I mean no.

jokes.text: On the floor! What a sense of humor! Stop it, Louie. You're making a jackass of yourself.
jokes.setup: Louie apparently throws himself on the floor dramatically
jokes.punchline: Zena's blunt assessment of his pathetic display
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie desperation move with Zena's direct response highlighting his patheticness.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm dying! I'm dying!

dialogue.speaker: Zena
dialogue.line: You're not dying, you're just being dramatic as usual.

jokes.text: You mean, no more... slave girl and the emperor.
jokes.setup: Zena threatens to cut off their relationship
jokes.punchline: Louie's reference to their apparent sexual roleplay
jokes.explanation: Perfectly cringey Louie moment - inappropriate oversharing that reveals his fantasy life.

dialogue.speaker: Zena
dialogue.line: That's it, Louie. I'm through with you. This relationship is over.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Zena, wait. Don't do this. I'll be good, I promise.

dialogue.speaker: Zena
dialogue.line: No, Louie. I've had it. I want someone who treats me with respect.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But baby, what about our little game? You know, where you're the angry dispatcher and I'm the desperate cabbie?

jokes.text: I'm a tough habit to break. You're a Louie addict. Nobody's been able to do it cold turkey.
jokes.setup: Louie tries to convince Zena she needs him
jokes.punchline: Compares himself to an addictive drug
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie delusion - turning his unpleasantness into a twisted form of self-aggrandizement.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You need me, Zena. You're addicted to me.

dialogue.speaker: Zena
dialogue.line: Addicted? To you? That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Think about it. I'm like a drug. A little bit makes you feel good, but you keep coming back for more.

dialogue.speaker: Zena
dialogue.line: The only thing you're addictive to is a strong stomach medication.

jokes.text: It must be hard on a guy like Louie, not having any friends to talk to when you're in trouble, you know? Yeah, well, who cares? Let's play.
jokes.setup: Jim expresses sympathy for Louie's isolation
jokes.punchline: Tony's immediate dismissal and desire to get back to cards
jokes.explanation: Nice character contrast - Jim's compassion immediately undercut by Tony's indifference.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's a shame about Louie. The guy's got nobody.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, you want to deal or what? We're losing daylight here.

jokes.text: All he needs to do is relax, rest his mind, get in touch with the oneness of the universe.
jokes.setup: Jim decides to help Louie with his problems
jokes.punchline: Applies his hippie philosophy to Louie's relationship troubles
jokes.explanation: Pure Jim character moment - his well-meaning but completely inappropriate spiritual advice.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know, Louie, I think your problem is that you're too caught up in material possessions and ego. What you need to do is center yourself, get in touch with your inner peace.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Jim, my wife left me.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: See, that's the attachment talking. You're clinging to the illusion of permanence. Everything is impermanent, man. Like a river flowing—

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: She took the car and the kids!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You gotta let it go, brother. Release your expectations. The universe will provide what you truly need.

jokes.text: Ooommm.
jokes.setup: Jim tries to lead Louie in meditation
jokes.punchline: Extended meditation chanting in Louie's office
jokes.explanation: Visual gag of Jim meditating while Louie presumably sits there - the humor is in the inappropriateness.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Now, Louie, I want you to close your eyes and clear your mind. We're going to achieve a state of perfect peace and tranquility.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever. Just make it snappy.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, now repeat after me. Ohhhhmmmmm...

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Ohhhhmmmmm...

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Ohhhhmmmmm...

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Ohhhhmmmmm...

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Ohhhhmmmmm...

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Ohhhhmmmmm...

jokes.text: It worked.
jokes.setup: After the meditation session ends
jokes.punchline: Jim's satisfied assessment while Louie is presumably unconscious
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim logic - thinking he's achieved enlightenment when he's probably just bored Louie into unconsciousness.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, I'd say that meditation session was a complete success. Louie's finally quiet.

jokes.text: Somebody get Sleeping Beauty out of here before I attach his tongue to a battery charger.
jokes.setup: Louie wakes up to find Jim still there
jokes.punchline: Threatens Jim with electrocution while calling him Sleeping Beauty
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie - combining a fairy tale reference with a violent threat in one perfectly nasty line.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What are you still doing here? You should've left hours ago.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I must've fallen asleep.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well wake up, Sleeping Beauty. And if you're not out of here in two minutes, I'm gonna plug you into that socket over there and see if you conduct electricity.

jokes.text: He's omed out.
jokes.setup: Jim is in a meditation stupor
jokes.punchline: Louie creates a drug reference pun with 'omed out'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that perfectly captures both the meditation and Louie's cynical view of Jim's hippie ways.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey, look at Jim. He's completely omed out.

jokes.text: Why? Because it's a fire drill. If you don't get out, you're all fired.
jokes.setup: Everyone questions why they have to leave
jokes.punchline: Louie's fire/fired pun as justification
jokes.explanation: Simple pun that shows Louie's ability to weaponize wordplay for intimidation.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You wanna know why you gotta leave? Because I said so. This is a fire zone.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And if you don't get outta here, you're fired.

jokes.text: You can spare another human being two minutes of your time. Yes, but how does that relate to this conversation?
jokes.setup: Louie appeals for compassion as a fellow human
jokes.punchline: Alex questions Louie's humanity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alex comeback - using Louie's own words against him with surgical precision.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Come on, Alex. We're both human beings here. Have a little compassion.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, I've seen you eat. You're not human.

jokes.text: Why? She's punishing them for a lousy childhood?
jokes.setup: Louie mentions Zena wants him to meet her parents
jokes.punchline: Alex suggests this would be punishment for the parents
jokes.explanation: Sharp insult disguised as concern for the parents - classic Alex wit.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Meet her parents? That's not a reward for you, that's a punishment for them.

jokes.text: Just the other day, I was thinking about kids and about a home and settling down, and suddenly, for the first time in my life, all those things didn't make me nauseous.
jokes.setup: Louie describes his newfound domestic desires
jokes.punchline: Frames not being nauseous as romantic progress
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - even his most romantic sentiment is expressed through physical revulsion.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about settling down, getting married, having a family. I want to come home to a nice dinner, sit by the fireplace with my wife...

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's wonderful, Louie. That's real progress.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you know what the best part is? When I think about it, I don't feel nauseous anymore.

jokes.text: Oh, that's beautiful. You've got one minute left.
jokes.setup: Louie shares his touching realization about love
jokes.punchline: Alex's sarcastic response followed by time limit reminder
jokes.explanation: Alex's dry response deflates Louie's moment perfectly while maintaining the scene's urgency.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about love, and I realized something beautiful. It's not about finding the perfect person, it's about appreciating the person you're with.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's nice, Louie. Really touching. Now get back to work before the dispatcher fires us both.

jokes.text: He's an exterminator? He's a minister.
jokes.setup: Louie mentions Zena's father has a profession hard for him to get along with
jokes.punchline: Alex guesses exterminator, Louie reveals minister - both would be problematic for Louie
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - both professions would be problems for Louie but for different reasons.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Exterminator?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Minister.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's worse than an exterminator?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, for me it is.

jokes.text: We have a longtime awareness that the other exists, and we know each other's names.
jokes.setup: Louie asks what they call their relationship
jokes.punchline: Alex's hilariously minimal definition of their connection
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode - perfectly captures the minimal nature of their relationship with clinical precision.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What do you call this relationship we have?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Employment.

jokes.text: By golly, my watch stopped.
jokes.setup: Alex counts down to refuse Louie's request
jokes.punchline: Pretends his countdown was interrupted by watch malfunction
jokes.explanation: Classic Alex - finds a face-saving way to help Louie while maintaining plausible deniability.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, I'm going to count to three, and by the time I reach three, I will have said no.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: One...

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Two...

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: My watch stopped.

jokes.text: I don't shake hands with anyone who's so close to my daughter. Oh, no, Mom, Mom. This is Louie.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Sherman greets who she thinks is Louie warmly
jokes.punchline: Reveals she was hugging Alex instead
jokes.explanation: Standard mistaken identity gag that sets up the fish-out-of-water dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: Oh Louie, darling! How I've missed you!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Actually, Mrs. Sherman, it's me. Alex.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: Alex? Oh my God!

jokes.text: I hope that Zena is as attractive as you in 60 years.
jokes.setup: Louie tries to compliment Mrs. Sherman
jokes.punchline: Accidentally implies she looks 60 years older than Zena
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie foot-in-mouth moment - well-intentioned but disastrously executed.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Mrs. Sherman, you look absolutely radiant today. Why, you don't look a day over 60.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: Louie, I'm 25.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, Zena's in the other room.

jokes.text: Strike one.
jokes.setup: Louie makes his age comment
jokes.punchline: Alex keeps score like a baseball game
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment where Alex frames Louie's social disasters as a sports competition.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's strike one, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Two more of those comments and you're out of here.

jokes.text: Besides, it's better to remain silent and be thought the fool than to speak and remove all doubt.
jokes.setup: Louie demands Alex say something classy
jokes.punchline: Alex quotes wisdom about staying quiet, perfectly describing Louie's situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect use of classic wisdom that doubles as commentary on Louie's behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Say something classy.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It is better to keep your mouth shut and appear foolish than to open it and remove all doubt.

jokes.text: Well, if it's not, your daughter is hugging a stranger.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Sherman asks if that's Nathan coming in
jokes.punchline: Nathan's matter-of-fact observation about what would be happening otherwise
jokes.explanation: Nice dry humor from the minister character - unexpected deadpan delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: Is that Nathan coming in?

dialogue.speaker: Nathan
dialogue.line: Well, if it wasn't me coming in, it would be someone else coming in, or nobody coming in at all.

jokes.text: That was some joke! Yes... I'm bunching up phlegm, here, it was so good.
jokes.setup: Nathan makes a mild dad joke
jokes.punchline: Louie's grossly over-enthusiastic and inappropriate response
jokes.explanation: Peak Louie awkwardness - trying too hard to connect and failing spectacularly with a disgusting metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's the funniest thing I've heard all week! You're killing me! Oh man, that's gold!

jokes.text: You can stand me up for God anytime.
jokes.setup: Nathan apologizes for being late
jokes.punchline: Louie's inappropriate attempt at religious humor
jokes.explanation: Another misguided Louie attempt at connecting through religion - awkward but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Nathan
dialogue.line: I'm sorry I'm late.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Late? You're not late. You know what late is? Late is when Jesus showed up three days after he was supposed to be crucified. Now that's late!

jokes.text: It's just that her antenna doesn't pick up every channel, if you know what I mean.
jokes.setup: Asked about his mother's mental state
jokes.punchline: Crude TV metaphor for mental illness
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie - inappropriate metaphor for a sensitive topic, delivered with complete lack of awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: My mother? She's like a TV set - half the time she's on the fritz, and the other half nobody knows what channel she's on.

jokes.text: Like the other night, she's laying in the bathtub... That's a mighty big bass you've got there, Mr. Sherman.
jokes.setup: Louie starts telling an inappropriate story about his mother
jokes.punchline: Alex desperately changes the subject to the fish on the wall
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic rescue - Alex's desperation to stop Louie's inappropriate story creates great tension.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So my mother, she's at the beauty parlor, right? And the beautician says to her...

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie! Louie, wait. Hey, did you ever notice that fish on the wall? That's a beautiful fish.

jokes.text: How do you keep a fish from smelling? I don't know. You cut off his nose.
jokes.setup: Nathan tells a fish joke
jokes.punchline: Classic dad joke with predictable punchline
jokes.explanation: Intentionally bad dad joke that works because it's exactly what you'd expect from a minister father trying to connect.

dialogue.speaker: Nathan
dialogue.line: Why did the fish blush?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I don't know, why?

dialogue.speaker: Nathan
dialogue.line: Because it saw the ocean's bottom!

jokes.text: Well, uh... now that we've exhausted that topic... does anybody want any more wine?
jokes.setup: After Nathan's terrible joke
jokes.punchline: Alex's polite but pointed observation about the joke's quality
jokes.explanation: Alex's diplomatic way of acknowledging the joke was terrible while keeping the conversation moving.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That joke was so bad, even the audience groaned before you finished the punchline.

jokes.text: In fact, my cousin Guido probably had his feet in this a couple of weeks ago.
jokes.setup: Louie mentions his family is in the wine business
jokes.punchline: Reveals the wine was made by feet-stomping
jokes.explanation: Another Louie overshare - inappropriate detail that kills any appetite for the wine.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: My family's in the wine business.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: We make it the old-fashioned way — with our feet.

jokes.text: I hope we don't have to have a race to the can after dinner.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Sherman mentions having indigestion
jokes.punchline: Louie's crude response about bathroom needs
jokes.explanation: Pure Louie - taking a polite mention of discomfort and making it aggressively crude.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: I have terrible indigestion.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, you're gonna have worse problems in about twenty minutes.

jokes.text: The fish again?
jokes.setup: Nathan brings up the bass again
jokes.punchline: Alex's exasperated recognition of the pattern
jokes.explanation: Nice callback showing Alex's growing frustration with the recurring fish topic.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Not the bass again!

jokes.text: But Zena is the first non-pro I've ever been out with.
jokes.setup: Louie tries to explain his dating history
jokes.punchline: Inadvertently reveals he's only dated prostitutes
jokes.explanation: Nuclear-level Louie moment - completely inappropriate revelation delivered with zero self-awareness to her parents.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I've had plenty of relationships. I've dated all kinds of women - short ones, tall ones, skinny ones, heavy ones.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The thing they all had in common? They all wanted money upfront.

jokes.text: Before I met Zena, the longest relationship I ever had was 30 minutes.
jokes.setup: Louie continues explaining his relationship experience
jokes.punchline: Makes the prostitute reference even more explicit
jokes.explanation: Doubles down on the inappropriate revelation, making it impossible to misinterpret.

jokes.text: You know what I'm talking about, huh, padre?
jokes.setup: Louie has just revealed his prostitute history
jokes.punchline: Directly addresses the minister with the implication
jokes.explanation: Makes the moment even worse by directly involving the minister in his crude implications.

jokes.text: This tomato is the best thing that ever happened to Louie De Palma.
jokes.setup: Louie tries to be romantic about Zena
jokes.punchline: Calls her 'tomato' in front of her parents
jokes.explanation: Another Louie misstep - using outdated slang that sounds disrespectful in this context.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Your daughter is a real tomato.

dialogue.speaker: Zena's Father
dialogue.line: What did you call her?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A tomato. You know, a real dish.

dialogue.speaker: Zena's Mother
dialogue.line: How dare you!

dialogue.speaker: Zena
dialogue.line: I told you he was trying to be romantic.

jokes.text: Do I have a choice?
jokes.setup: Zena 'volunteers' Louie to help her mother
jokes.punchline: Louie's recognition that it's not really voluntary
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - shows Louie's awareness of his precarious position.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. You volunteered me? That's not volunteering. That's volun-telling!

jokes.text: I'm sorry, Beth, I already have a girl.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Sherman says she wants to talk to him
jokes.punchline: Louie misinterprets as a romantic proposition
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie ego - assumes every woman wants him despite evidence to the contrary.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: Louie, I'd like to talk to you.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh, Mrs. Sherman! Finally! I've been waiting for this moment. Come here.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: No, Louie, I just want to discuss—

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Don't be shy. I know what you want to talk about.

jokes.text: Because if you do, I'll have you killed.
jokes.setup: Sweet motherly advice about not getting too serious
jokes.punchline: Casual death threat from the minister's wife
jokes.explanation: Stunning reversal - the sweet minister's wife reveals a murderously protective side. Perfect dark comedy moment.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: You know, you shouldn't get too serious about things. Life is short, and you should enjoy yourself while you can.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: But if you ever hurt my boy, I'll kill you.

jokes.text: You can hire people to do that for you, can't you? Yeah. Now, what do you call them? Hit men.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Sherman has just threatened to have Louie killed
jokes.punchline: She casually asks Louie for assassination advice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - forces Louie to help plan his own potential murder while maintaining sweet grandmotherly tone.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: You know, I've decided to have you killed.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What? You're gonna have me killed?

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: Yes. Do you have any suggestions on how I should do it?

jokes.text: You can hire people to do that for you, can't you? Yeah. Now, what do you call them? Hit men.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Sherman continues her murder research
jokes.punchline: Louie helpfully providing the terminology for hired killers
jokes.explanation: Hilarious that Louie is helpfully educating the woman planning his potential murder.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: You know, I've been reading about all these different ways people get murdered.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, if you're looking to hire someone, they call that a 'contract killer.'

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: Contract killer? I thought that was someone who breaks their promises.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No, no. A contract killer is a professional. You pay 'em, they do the job.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: Oh. What if you want someone who's a little less professional?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Then you call an amateur. We got a few of those driving cabs.

jokes.text: But you know, it would probably ruin everything I've ever tried to do with my life. And poor Nathan! He would be destroyed, but it would be worth it, don't you think?
jokes.setup: Continues planning Louie's murder
jokes.punchline: Weighs the consequences but decides it would be worth it
jokes.explanation: Continues the brilliant character reversal - maternal love turned homicidal, delivered with sweet reasonableness.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about it. If I killed Louie, they'd probably give me life in prison. But you know what? It would be worth it.

jokes.text: Switch with me. Huh? Switch with me. Why? Just do it.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Sherman serves coffee
jokes.punchline: Louie frantically tries to avoid drinking what she gave him
jokes.explanation: Perfect paranoid callback - Louie now fears she's already trying to poison him.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: Here's your coffee, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. What is this?

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: It's coffee. What do you think?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I'm not drinking this. This doesn't look right.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What's wrong with it?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Wrong with it? Look at it! It's moving!

jokes.text: I assure you, I meant everything I said from the bottom of my heart.
jokes.setup: Seems like innocent farewell sentiment
jokes.punchline: Actually referring to her murder threat
jokes.explanation: Chilling callback that works on two levels - sounds sweet to others but terrifying to Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sherman
dialogue.line: I'm going to kill you.

jokes.text: No, no, we... No. No.
jokes.setup: Zena suggests her mother give Louie a goodbye kiss
jokes.punchline: Louie's terrified refusal
jokes.explanation: Final callback showing Louie's complete terror of Mrs. Sherman after her threats.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No! No! I don't want a goodbye kiss from your mother!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The last woman who kissed me goodbye, I ended up married to her for three years!

jokes.text: See? And you were worried.
jokes.setup: The evening has ended
jokes.punchline: Zena's oblivious assessment that everything went well
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic ending - Zena has no idea how badly things actually went.

dialogue.speaker: Zena
dialogue.line: Well, I think that went very well! Everyone seemed to have a wonderful time.

tmdb_overview: Nervous about meeting his girlfriend's parents, Louie hires Alex for moral support.
insight: Louie's parental dinner spirals through 52 jokes at measured pace, leaning on character discomfort over punchlines.
